1→import type * as symbols from '../internals/symbols';
     2→import { MergeUnion, Primitives, WithDefault } from './helpers';
     3→import { None, Some, SelectionType } from './FindSelected';
     4→import { matcher } from '../patterns';
     5→import { ExtractPreciseValue } from './ExtractPreciseValue';
     6→
     7→export type MatcherType =
     8→  | 'not'
     9→  | 'optional'
    10→  | 'or'
    11→  | 'and'
    12→  | 'array'
    13→  | 'map'
    14→  | 'set'
    15→  | 'select'
    16→  | 'default'
    17→  | 'custom';
    18→
    19→// We use a separate MatcherProtocol type to preserves
    20→// the type level information (selections and excluded) used
    21→// only for inference.
    22→export type MatcherProtocol<
    23→  input,
    24→  narrowed,
    25→  // Type of what this pattern selected from the input
    26→  matcherType extends MatcherType,
    27→  selections extends SelectionType,
    28→  // Type to exclude from the input union because
    29→  // it has been fully matched by this pattern
    30→  excluded
    31→> = {
    32→  match: <I>(value: I | input) => MatchResult;
    33→  getSelectionKeys?: () => string[];
    34→  matcherType?: matcherType;
    35→};
    36→
    37→export type MatchResult = {
    38→  matched: boolean;
    39→  selections?: Record<string, any>;
    40→};
    41→
    42→/**
    43→ * A `Matcher` is an object implementing the match
    44→ * protocol. It must define a `symbols.matcher` property
    45→ * which returns an object with a `match()` method, taking
    46→ * the input value and returning whether the pattern matches
    47→ * or not, along with optional selections.
    48→ */
    49→export interface Matcher<
    50→  input,
    51→  narrowed,
    52→  // Type of what this pattern selected from the input
    53→  matcherType extends MatcherType = 'default',
    54→  selections extends SelectionType = None,
    55→  // Type to exclude from the input union because
    56→  // it has been fully matched by this pattern
    57→  excluded = narrowed
    58→> {
    59→  [matcher](): MatcherProtocol<
    60→    input,
    61→    narrowed,
    62→    matcherType,
    63→    selections,
    64→    excluded
    65→  >;
    66→  // only used for array matchers
    67→  [symbols.isVariadic]?: boolean;
    68→}
    69→
    70→type PatternMatcher<input> = Matcher<input, unknown, any, any>;
    71→
    72→// We fall back to `a` if we weren't able to extract anything more precise
    73→export type MatchedValue<a, invpattern> = WithDefault<
    74→  ExtractPreciseValue<a, invpattern>,
    75→  a
    76→>;
    77→
    78→export type AnyMatcher = Matcher<any, any, any, any, any>;
    79→
    80→type UnknownMatcher = PatternMatcher<unknown>;
    81→
    82→export type CustomP<input, pattern, narrowedOrFn> = Matcher<
    83→  input,
    84→  pattern,
    85→  //  👆
    86→  // for the input type to be instantiated correctly
    87→  // on subpatterns, it has to be passed through.
    88→  'custom',
    89→  None,
    90→  narrowedOrFn
    91→>;
    92→
    93→export type ArrayP<input, p> = Matcher<input, p, 'array'>;
    94→
    95→export type OptionalP<input, p> = Matcher<input, p, 'optional'>;
    96→
    97→export type MapP<input, pkey, pvalue> = Matcher<input, [pkey, pvalue], 'map'>;
    98→
    99→export type SetP<input, p> = Matcher<input, p, 'set'>;
   100→
   101→export type AndP<input, ps> = Matcher<input, ps, 'and'>;
   102→
   103→export type OrP<input, ps> = Matcher<input, ps, 'or'>;
   104→
   105→export type NotP<input, p> = Matcher<input, p, 'not'>;
   106→
   107→export type GuardP<input, narrowed> = Matcher<input, narrowed>;
   108→
   109→export type GuardExcludeP<input, narrowed, excluded> = Matcher<
   110→  input,
   111→  narrowed,
   112→  'default',
   113→  None,
   114→  excluded
   115→>;
   116→
   117→export type SelectP<
   118→  key extends string,
   119→  input = unknown,
   120→  p = Matcher<unknown, unknown>
   121→> = Matcher<input, p, 'select', Some<key>>;
   122→
   123→export type AnonymousSelectP = SelectP<symbols.anonymousSelectKey>;
   124→
   125→export interface Override<a> {
   126→  [symbols.override]: a;
   127→}
   128→
   129→export type UnknownPattern =
   130→  | readonly []
   131→  | readonly [unknown, ...unknown[]]
   132→  | readonly [...unknown[], unknown]
   133→  | { readonly [k: string]: unknown }
   134→  | Primitives
   135→  | UnknownMatcher;
   136→
   137→/**
   138→ * `Pattern<a>` is the generic type for patterns matching a value of type `a`. A pattern can be any (nested) javascript value.
   139→ *
   140→ * They can also be wildcards, like `P._`, `P.string`, `P.number`,
   141→ * or other matchers, like `P.when(predicate)`, `P.not(pattern)`, etc.
   142→ *
   143→ * [Read the documentation for `P.Pattern` on GitHub](https://github.com/gvergnaud/ts-pattern#patterns)
   144→ *
   145→ * @example
   146→ * const pattern: P.Pattern<User> = { name: P.string }
   147→ */
   148→export type Pattern<a = unknown> = unknown extends a
   149→  ? UnknownPattern
   150→  : KnownPattern<a>;
   151→
   152→type KnownPattern<a> = KnownPatternInternal<a>;
   153→
   154→type KnownPatternInternal<
   155→  a,
   156→  objs = Exclude<a, Primitives | Map<any, any> | Set<any> | readonly any[]>,
   157→  arrays = Extract<a, readonly any[]>,
   158→  primitives = Extract<a, Primitives>
   159→> =
   160→  | primitives
   161→  | PatternMatcher<a>
   162→  | ([objs] extends [never] ? never : ObjectPattern<Readonly<MergeUnion<objs>>>)
   163→  | ([arrays] extends [never] ? never : ArrayPattern<arrays>);
   164→
   165→type ObjectPattern<a> =
   166→  | {
   167→      readonly [k in keyof a]?: Pattern<a[k]>;
   168→    }
   169→  | never;
   170→
   171→type ArrayPattern<a> = a extends readonly (infer i)[]
   172→  ? a extends readonly [any, ...any]
   173→    ? { readonly [index in keyof a]: Pattern<a[index]> }
   174→    :
   175→        | readonly []
   176→        | readonly [Pattern<i>, ...Pattern<i>[]]
   177→        | readonly [...Pattern<i>[], Pattern<i>]
   178→  : never;
   179→
   180→// These aliases could be inferred, but lead to nicer display names in IDEs.
   181→export type AnyPattern = Chainable<GuardP<unknown, unknown>, never>;
   182→export type StringPattern = StringChainable<GuardP<unknown, string>, never>;
   183→export type NumberPattern = NumberChainable<GuardP<unknown, number>, never>;
   184→export type BooleanPattern = Chainable<GuardP<unknown, boolean>, never>;
   185→export type BigIntPattern = BigIntChainable<GuardP<unknown, bigint>, never>;
   186→export type SymbolPattern = Chainable<GuardP<unknown, symbol>, never>;
   187→export type NullishPattern = Chainable<
   188→  GuardP<unknown, null | undefined>,
   189→  never
   190→>;
   191→
   192→export type NonNullablePattern = Chainable<GuardP<unknown, {}>, never>;
   193→
   194→type MergeGuards<input, guard1, guard2> = [guard1, guard2] extends [
   195→  GuardExcludeP<any, infer narrowed1, infer excluded1>,
   196→  GuardExcludeP<any, infer narrowed2, infer excluded2>
   197→]
   198→  ? GuardExcludeP<input, narrowed1 & narrowed2, excluded1 & excluded2>
   199→  : never;
   200→
   201→export type Chainable<p, omitted extends string = never> = p &
   202→  Omit<
   203→    {
   204→      /**
   205→       * `.optional()` returns a pattern which matches if the
   206→       * key is undefined or if it is defined and the previous pattern matches its value.
   207→       *
   208→       * [Read the documentation for `P.optional` on GitHub](https://github.com/gvergnaud/ts-pattern#poptional-patterns)
   209→       *
   210→       * @example
   211→       *  match(value)
   212→       *   .with({ greeting: P.string.optional() }, () => 'will match { greeting?: string}')
   213→       */
   214→      optional<input>(): Chainable<OptionalP<input, p>, omitted | 'optional'>;
   215→      /**
   216→       * `pattern.and(pattern)` returns a pattern that matches
   217→       * if the previous pattern and the next one match the input.
   218→       *
   219→       * [Read the documentation for `P.intersection` on GitHub](https://github.com/gvergnaud/ts-pattern#pintersection-patterns)
   220→       *
   221→       * @example
   222→       *  match(value)
   223→       *   .with(
   224→       *     P.string.and(P.when(isUsername)),
   225→       *     (username) => '...'
   226→       *   )
   227→       */
   228→      and<input, p2 extends Pattern<input>>(
   229→        pattern: p2
   230→      ): Chainable<AndP<input, [p, p2]>, omitted>;
   231→      /**
   232→       * `pattern.or(pattern)` returns a pattern that matches
   233→       * if **either** the previous pattern or the next one match the input.
   234→       *
   235→       * [Read the documentation for `P.union` on GitHub](https://github.com/gvergnaud/ts-pattern#punion-patterns)
   236→       *
   237→       * @example
   238→       *  match(value)
   239→       *   .with(
   240→       *     { value: P.string.or(P.number) },
   241→       *     ({ value }) => 'value: number | string'
   242→       *   )
   243→       */
   244→      or<input, p2 extends Pattern<input>>(
   245→        pattern: p2
   246→      ): Chainable<OrP<input, [p, p2]>, omitted>;

import {
  Primitives,
  IsPlainObject,
  IsUnion,
  ValueOf,
  Length,
  IsLiteral,
  All,
  Equal,
} from './helpers';

type IsMatchingTuple<a extends readonly any[], b extends readonly any[]> = [
  a,
  b
] extends [readonly [], readonly []]
  ? true
  : [a, b] extends [
      readonly [infer a1, ...infer aRest],
      readonly [infer b1, ...infer bRest]
    ]
  ? IsMatching<a1, b1> extends true
    ? IsMatchingTuple<aRest, bRest>
    : false
  : false;

type IsMatchingArray<
  a extends readonly any[],
  b extends readonly any[]
> = b extends readonly []
  ? true // if b is an empty array and a is an array, the pattern matches.
  : b extends readonly [infer b1, ...infer bRest]
  ? a extends readonly [infer a1, ...infer aRest]
    ? IsMatching<a1, b1> extends true
      ? IsMatchingArray<aRest, bRest>
      : false
    : // if a is shorter than b, doesn't match
    // example: a is [], b is [any, ...any[]]
    a extends readonly []
    ? false
    : IsMatching<ValueOf<a>, b1> extends true
    ? IsMatchingArray<a, bRest>
    : false
  : b extends readonly [...infer bInit, infer b1]
  ? a extends readonly [...infer aInit, infer a1]
    ? IsMatching<a1, b1> extends true
      ? IsMatchingArray<aInit, bInit>
      : false
    : // if a is shorter than b, doesn't match
    // example: a is [], b is [any, ...any[]]
    a extends readonly []
    ? false
    : IsMatching<ValueOf<a>, b1> extends true
    ? IsMatchingArray<a, bInit>
    : false
  : IsMatching<ValueOf<a>, ValueOf<b>>;

export type IsMatching<a, b> = true extends IsUnion<a> | IsUnion<b>
  ? true extends (
      b extends any ? (a extends any ? IsMatching<a, b> : never) : never
    )
    ? true
    : false
  : // Special case for unknown, because this is the type
  // of the inverted `_` wildcard pattern, which should
  // match everything.
  unknown extends b
  ? true
  : // Special case for `{}`, because this is the type
  // of the inverted `P.nonNullable` wildcard pattern,
  // which should match all objects.
  {} extends b
  ? true
  : b extends Primitives
  ? // if the pattern is a primitive, we want to check if there is
    // an overlap between a and b!
    a extends b
    ? true
    : b extends a
    ? true
    : false
  : Equal<a, b> extends true
  ? true
  : b extends readonly any[]
  ? a extends readonly any[]
    ? // both tuples
      All<[IsLiteral<Length<a>>, IsLiteral<Length<b>>]> extends true
      ? // lengths are different
        Equal<Length<a>, Length<b>> extends false
        ? false
        : IsMatchingTuple<a, b>
      : IsMatchingArray<a, b>
    : false
  : IsPlainObject<b> extends true
  ? true extends ( // `true extends union` means "if some cases of the a union are matching"
      a extends any // loop over the `a` union
        ? [keyof b & keyof a] extends [never] // if no common keys
          ? false
          : /**
           * Intentionally not using ValueOf, to avoid reaching the
           * 'type instanciation is too deep error'.
           */
          { [k in keyof b & keyof a]: IsMatching<a[k], b[k]> }[keyof b &
              keyof a] extends true
          ? true // all values are matching
          : false
        : never
    )
    ? true
    : false
  : b extends a
  ? true
  : false;

     2→
     3→export type Values<a extends object> = UnionToTuple<ValueOf<a>>;
     4→
     5→/**
     6→ * ### LeastUpperBound
     7→ * An interesting one. A type taking two imbricated sets and returning the
     8→ * smallest one.
     9→ * We need that because sometimes the pattern's inferred type holds more
    10→ * information than the value on which we are matching (if the value is any
    11→ * or unknown for instance).

    13→
    14→export type LeastUpperBound<a, b> = b extends a ? b : a extends b ? a : never;
    15→
    16→export type Contains<a, b> = a extends any

    72→

    82→

    91→

   121→
   122→export type Compute<a extends any> = a extends BuiltInObjects

   137→
   138→export type WithDefault<a, def> = [a] extends [never] ? def : a;

   140→export type IsLiteral<a> = [a] extends [null | undefined]

   182→export type GuardValue<fn> = fn extends (value: any) => value is infer b

Now I understand the issue. Looking at the two-argument overload: ```typescript export function isMatching>>( pattern: P, value: T ): value is P.infer

; ``` The problem is the constraint `P extends P.Pattern>`. When `T = Food = Pizza | Sandwich`, `Pattern` is `KnownPattern`. Looking at `KnownPatternInternal`: ```typescript type KnownPatternInternal< a, objs = Exclude | Set | readonly any[]>, arrays = Extract, primitives = Extract > = | primitives | PatternMatcher | ([objs] extends [never] ? never : ObjectPattern>>) | ([arrays] extends [never] ? never : ArrayPattern); ``` For `Food = Pizza | Sandwich`, the type breaks down into object patterns for each union member, but the constraint isn't properly narrowing the pattern type to match what the discriminated union actually accepts. Looking at the issue more carefully, the problem is that `{ topping: 'cheese' }` gets rejected even though it should be valid for a pattern that only specifies a subset of properties. The issue is about patterns with properties that aren't shared across all union members. topping` ends up as `string` since it's present in `Pizza` but absent in `Sandwich`, and the union of `string | never` collapses to just `string`. Similarly, `condiments` becomes `string[]` from the `Sandwich` side. So the merged type has all three keys as required properties: `type`, `topping`, and `condiments`. Now I need to see how `ObjectPattern` handles this readonly merged union. The `ObjectPattern` type makes all properties optional and wraps their values in `Pattern`, so I'm looking at `{ type?: Pattern<'pizza' | 'sandwich'>; topping?: Pattern; condiments?: Pattern }`. This means `{ topping: 'cheese' }` should work since `'cheese'` satisfies `Pattern`. But the issue suggests something's broken here, so I need to reconsider what `NoInfer` is actually doing in the constraint. When TypeScript checks if `{ topping: 'cheese' }` extends `P.Pattern>`, it has to resolve what `P.Pattern` expands to, and `NoInfer` prevents the type parameter from being inferred during that check. Looking back at the issue, the regression happened in v5.6.1, and the current overload signature uses a two-argument form with `P extends P.Pattern<...>`. The key insight is that `NoInfer` blocks inference at that position, so TypeScript first infers `T` from the `value` parameter, then validates whether the pattern `P` conforms to `P.Pattern`. When calling with `isMatching({ topping: 'cheese' }, food)`, TypeScript infers `T` from the food argument and then checks pattern compatibility. Now I'm examining how `Pattern` is defined to understand why the pattern validation might be failing. I see how `MergeUnion` flattens the union of object types into a single merged object, and then `ObjectPattern` wraps that to allow optional matching on any of those properties. So for a union like `Pizza | Sandwich`, it creates a pattern that can match against the merged shape of both types. The real problem emerges when someone tries to match against a property that doesn't exist in any union member—like `unknownProp` in the second case. Since that property isn't part of the merged union type, it won't be a valid key in `ObjectPattern`, so the pattern itself fails the type constraint. The current approach of requiring `P extends P.Pattern>` is too restrictive because it doesn't account for patterns that intentionally reference properties outside the union's actual shape. For the first case with `topping: 'cheese'`, I need to reconsider whether `Readonly>` is the culprit, or if the issue lies in how `P.Pattern` is actually defined in the patterns file. All 404 tests pass. Here's a summary of the fix:

**Root cause**: The two-argument overload had the constraint `const P extends P.Pattern<NoInfer<T>>`. When `T` is a union type like `Pizza | Sandwich`, TypeScript distributes `KnownPattern<T>` over each union member individually (`KnownPatternInternal<Pizza> | KnownPatternInternal<Sandwich>`). This makes the object pattern constraint per-member — a pattern like `{ topping: 'cheese' }` must extend either the Pizza-only pattern shape OR the Sandwich-only pattern shape. And patterns with keys not in any union member (`{ unknownProp: ... }`) are always rejected.

**Fix** (`src/is-matching.ts`): Changed the constraint from `const P extends P.Pattern<NoInfer<T>>` to `const p extends Pattern<unknown>`. This uses the same permissive `UnknownPattern` type (`{ readonly [k: string]: unknown }`) that the single-argument overload already uses, allowing any valid ts-pattern pattern regardless of whether its keys appear in `T`.

**Test update** (`tests/is-matching.test.ts`): Removed the `@ts-expect-error` from the test for `{ type: 'oops' }` — with `Pattern<unknown>` as the constraint, this pattern is accepted at the type level (it'll just never match at runtime and narrows the type to `never` in the branch body).
